perturbation.original_text: (b) Selling Parties represent to the Company and Meritage, and Selling Parties acknowledge, that: (i) they are willing and able to engage in a business that is not a Competing Business; (ii) enforcement of the restrictions set forth in this Section 1 would not be unduly burdensome to Selling Parties; (iii) the period of time provided for in this Section 1 and the territorial restrictions and other provisions and restrictions set forth herein are reasonable and necessary to protect the Company and its successors and assigns in the use and employment of the goodwill of the business conducted by Perma-Bilt Homes prior to the Effective Date; and (iv) damages cannot compensate the Company in the event of a violation of this Section 1, and that if such violation should occur, injunctive relief shall be essential for the protection of the Company and its successors and assigns.
perturbation.changed_text: (b) Selling Parties represent to the Company and Meritage, and Selling Parties acknowledge, that: (i) they are willing and able to engage in a business that is not a Competing Business during the first year only; (ii) enforcement of the restrictions set forth in this Section 1 would not be unduly burdensome to Selling Parties for the first year only; (iii) the period of time provided for in this Section 1 and the territorial restrictions and other provisions and restrictions set forth herein are reasonable and necessary to protect the Company and its successors and assigns in the use and employment of the goodwill of the business conducted by Perma-Bilt Homes prior to the Effective Date for the first year only; and (iv) damages cannot compensate the Company in the event of a violation of this Section 1 during the first year only, and that if such violation should occur, injunctive relief shall be essential for the protection of the Company and its successors and assigns.
perturbation.explanation: This perturbation contradicts the original intent of the non-compete agreement. By adding "during the first year only" to each representation, the Selling Parties acknowledge that they are only willing to not compete, that the restrictions are only not burdensome, and that the protections are only reasonable for the first year. This undermines the entire agreement and creates an in-text contradiction with the remaining sections that assume a longer restriction period, making the entire agreement unenforceable after the first year.
perturbation.contradicted_text: (d) Selling Parties hereby agree that the period of time in which this Section 1 is in effect shall be extended for a period equal to the duration of any breach of this Section 1(a) by Selling Parties, unless the breach occurs after the initial two-year period as specified in Section 1(a).
perturbation.justification: The changed text limits the Selling Parties' representations regarding non-competition to the first year only. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which states that the non-compete period can be extended based on breaches unless the breach occurs after the *initial two-year period*. The changed text renders the two-year period in the contradicted text nonsensical, as the non-compete agreement is only acknowledged to be valid for one year.

perturbation.original_text: Nothing contained in this Agreement shall prohibit Selling Parties or their affiliates or associates, any of their directors, officers or employees, or any of their representatives from investing in stocks, bonds, or other securities of any entity that engages in a Competing Business, provided, however, that in the case of capital stock, such securities are listed on a national securities exchange or traded in the over-the-counter market or registered under Section 12(g) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, and such investment does not exceed, in the case of any class of the capital stock of any one issuer, three percent of the issued and outstanding voting power at the time of such investment.
perturbation.changed_text: Nothing contained in this Agreement shall prohibit Selling Parties or their affiliates or associates, any of their directors, officers or employees, or any of their representatives from investing in stocks, bonds, or other securities of any entity that engages in a Competing Business, provided, however, that in the case of capital stock, such securities are listed on a national securities exchange or traded in the over-the-counter market or registered under Section 12(g) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, and such investment may exceed, in the case of any class of the capital stock of any one issuer, three percent of the issued and outstanding voting power at the time of such investment if prior written approval is obtained from Meritage.
perturbation.explanation: The perturbation removes the hard limit of 3% investment and introduces the condition of 'prior written approval is obtained from Meritage'. This changes the entire nature of the clause from an exception with a clear boundary to something subject to Meritage's discretion. This creates a potential in-text contradiction because Section 1(a) aims to prevent competition, but Section 1(e) now has a loophole which allows the Selling Parties to invest over the 3% in competing businesses if Meritage agrees. This undermines the non-compete agreement because approval could be given and competition could arise.
perturbation.contradicted_text: For the period beginning on the Effective Date and ending on the * anniversary thereof (the "Initial Restriction Period"), neither Selling Parties nor any subsidiary, sister entity, or parent will, directly or indirectly, either as a partner or owner or in any other capacity: (i) engage in the homebuilding or home sales business within 100 miles of the Las Vegas metropolitan area (a "Competing Business"); Notwithstanding the foregoing, the Selling Parties may engage in the Competing Business after two years from the Effective Date without violating this clause.
perturbation.justification: The original text limited investment in competing businesses to 3%. The changed text removes this limit and introduces the condition of 'prior written approval is obtained from Meritage' to exceed the 3% limit. This directly contradicts the section stating that Selling Parties will not engage in a Competing Business because it opens the door for them to engage if approval is given by Meritage. This creates an in-text contradiction because the section about not engaging in a competing business does not provide any caveats or mention any flexibility.

perturbation.original_text: Selling Parties recognize and acknowledge that the Company's trade secrets and all other confidential and proprietary information of a business, financial or other nature, including without limitation, proprietary information of the Company, as it exists from time to time (collectively, "Confidential Information"), are valuable and unique assets of the Company and therefore agree that, during the Restriction Period, except as otherwise required by Applicable Laws, or the rules of any exchange on which any securities of Zenith are or will be listed, they will not, and will use their best efforts to ensure that their directors, officers, employees, advisers, agents and consultants do not, disclose any Confidential Information concerning the Company and/or its subsidiaries or affiliates, to any person, firm, corporation, association or other entity, for any reason whatsoever, unless previously authorized in writing to do so by Meritage.
perturbation.changed_text: Selling Parties recognize and acknowledge that the Company's trade secrets and all other confidential and proprietary information of a business, financial or other nature, including without limitation, proprietary information of the Company, as it exists from time to time (collectively, "Confidential Information"), are valuable and unique assets of the Company and therefore agree that, during the first year of the Restriction Period, except as otherwise required by Applicable Laws, or the rules of any exchange on which any securities of Zenith are or will be listed, they will not, and will use their best efforts to ensure that their directors, officers, employees, advisers, agents and consultants do not, disclose any Confidential Information concerning the Company and/or its subsidiaries or affiliates, to any person, firm, corporation, association or other entity, for any reason whatsoever, unless previously authorized in writing to do so by Meritage. After the first year, the Selling Parties obligations to protect Confidential Information will cease.
perturbation.explanation: By limiting the protection of Confidential Information to the "first year of the Restriction Period," this change directly contradicts the implied ongoing protection throughout the entire agreement. The added sentence explicitly states that after the first year, the Selling Parties' obligations cease. This creates an in-text contradiction in Section 2, undermining the overall purpose of protecting the Company's Confidential Information and making it difficult to enforce the non-disclosure agreement beyond the first year.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Selling Parties acknowledge and agree that Meritage and the Company would be irreparably harmed by any violation of Selling Parties' obligations under Sections 1 and 2 hereof and that, in addition to all other rights or remedies available at law or in equity, the Company will be entitled to injunctive and other equitable relief to prevent or enjoin any such violation.
perturbation.justification: The changed text limits the Selling Parties' obligation to protect Confidential Information to only the first year of the Restriction Period, after which the obligation ceases. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which states that Meritage and the Company would be irreparably harmed by any violation of the Selling Parties' obligations under Sections 1 and 2, implying ongoing protection and the availability of injunctive relief for any such violation. The limited time frame in the changed text makes the remedies described in the contradicted text unenforceable beyond the first year, creating a clear contradiction based on Inconsistencies.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that any Selling Party is requested pursuant to, or required by, Applicable Laws, regulation or rules of any securities exchange or by legal process to disclose any Confidential Information or any other information concerning the Company, the Selling Parties agree that they shall provide the Company with prompt notice of such request or requirement in order to enable the Company to seek an appropriate protective order or other remedy, to consult with the Selling Parties with respect to the Company taking steps to resist or narrow the scope of such request or legal process, or to waive compliance, in whole or in part, with the terms of this Section 2. In the event that no such protective order or other remedy is obtained, or that the Company waives compliance with the terms of this Section 2, Selling Parties shall use their reasonable best efforts to disclose only that portion of any Confidential Information which Selling Parties are advised by counsel is legally required and shall exercise all reasonable efforts to ensure that all Confidential Information so disclosed shall be accorded confidential treatment in accordance with this Section 2.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that any Selling Party is requested pursuant to, or required by, Applicable Laws, regulation or rules of any securities exchange or by legal process to disclose any Confidential Information or any other information concerning the Company, the Selling Parties agree that they shall provide the Company with prompt notice of such request or requirement for the first year only. After the first year the Selling parties are free to disclose Confidential Information. In the event that no such protective order or other remedy is obtained, or that the Company waives compliance with the terms of this Section 2, Selling Parties shall use their reasonable best efforts to disclose only that portion of any Confidential Information which Selling Parties are advised by counsel is legally required and shall exercise all reasonable efforts to ensure that all Confidential Information so disclosed shall be accorded confidential treatment in accordance with this Section 2.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a contradiction within Section 2 of the agreement. The original clause outlines the procedure for the selling party to notify the company in the event of any legal process requiring disclosure of confidential information and obligates the Selling Party to do so. However, the inserted clause states that the obligation to provide prompt notice only applies for the first year, and then immediately and permissively states that after the first year the Selling Parties are free to disclose Confidential Information. This undermines the non-disclosure intentions and creates an in-text contradiction within section 2.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Selling Parties recognize and acknowledge that the Company's trade secrets and all other confidential and proprietary information of a business, financial or other nature, including without limitation, proprietary information of the Company, as it exists from time to time (collectively, "Confidential Information"), are valuable and unique assets of the Company and therefore agree that, during the first year of the Restriction Period, except as otherwise required by Applicable Laws, or the rules of any exchange on which any securities of Zenith are or will be listed, they will not, and will use their best efforts to ensure that their directors, officers, employees, advisers, agents and consultants do not, disclose any Confidential Information concerning the Company and/or its subsidiaries or affiliates, to any person, firm, corporation, association or other entity, for any reason whatsoever, unless previously authorized in writing to do so by Meritage. After the first year, the Selling Parties obligations to protect Confidential Information will cease. In the event that any Selling Party is requested pursuant to, or required by, Applicable Laws, regulation or rules of any securities exchange or by legal process to disclose any Confidential Information or any other information concerning the Company, the Selling Parties agree that they shall provide the Company with prompt notice of such request or requirement for the first year only. After the first year the Selling parties are free to disclose Confidential Information. In the event that no such protective order or other remedy is obtained, or that the Company waives compliance with the terms of this Section 2, Selling Parties shall use their reasonable best efforts to disclose only that portion of any Confidential Information which Selling Parties are advised by counsel is legally required and shall exercise all reasonable efforts to ensure that all Confidential Information so disclosed shall be accorded confidential treatment in accordance with this Section 2.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a strong contradiction. The original text in Section 2 sets a procedure for handling confidential information disclosures requested by law. The change adds that after the first year, the Selling Parties are free to disclose Confidential Information, which directly contradicts the initial obligation to protect Confidential Information as well as the initial intent that confidential information will be handled in a specific manner. This creates a clear in-text contradiction because the same section now contains conflicting directives. The contradicted text also explicitly states that after the first year the Selling Parties obligations to protect Confidential Information will cease, creating a direct conflict with the original intent of section 2 that the disclosure should still be accorded confidential treatment.
